subject: Deep space relay losing coherence — navigation data arriving garbled
description: The primary deep space relay between CDSS and the Proxima Centauri beacon has been losing signal coherence for the past three hours. Navigation correction data for ships in transit is arriving garbled — coordinates are off by 0.003 degrees, which doesn't sound like much until you realize that at interstellar distances, 0.003 degrees is the difference between 'arriving at the colony' and 'arriving at a very large, very empty patch of space where the colony isn't.' Two resupply freighters and one passenger transport are currently relying on this relay for mid-course corrections. The relay worked fine until last Tuesday's firmware push — you know, the one that updated relay software across the entire sector without asking anyone who actually uses the relay whether that was a good idea. SubComm v3.2 on Deck 4 relay station. The old firmware was fine. The new firmware has opinions about signal encoding that differ from reality. I've been manually correcting the navigation data by hand, which is working for now, but I'd prefer the relay to do its one job before I make a math error and a freighter ends up in the Oort Cloud wondering what went wrong.

subject: Proximity alert showing phantom asteroids + collision avoidance calculating evasion for ghosts
description: Two problems since yesterday's sensor array recalibration, and I'm not sure which one is going to kill us first: (1) The proximity alert system is flagging asteroids that don't exist. Long-range sensors show clear space, but the short-range proximity grid is painting the void with phantom contacts like it's hosting a rock concert for imaginary space debris. The observation deck crew nearly initiated emergency evacuation because the display showed an asteroid the size of a small moon bearing down on us. There is no asteroid. There has never been an asteroid. The asteroid is a lie. (2) The automated collision avoidance system doesn't know the asteroids aren't real, so it keeps calculating evasion vectors and requesting permission to fire thrusters. If someone accidentally authorizes one of those maneuvers, we'll burn fuel dodging nothing and end up in a completely unplanned orbit. Both started immediately after the sensor recalibration. Might be a calibration offset. Might be something worse. Either way, my crew is developing anxiety disorders from phantom asteroids and I'd like that to stop.

subject: Observation deck viewport micro-fracture — Admiral arriving in 30 minutes
description: The main observation deck viewport just failed its automated pressure integrity diagnostic. The system is reporting a micro-fracture in the lower-left quadrant of panel VP-7. Now, this could be a sensor false positive — the integrity sensors on the observation deck have cried wolf before, usually right before an inspection. But it could also be a real micro-fracture, in which case we are looking at a viewport that is one bad thermal cycle away from explosive decompression. The Admiral and a delegation from Allied Fleet Command are arriving via shuttle in approximately 30 minutes for the diplomatic reception. The observation deck is the ONLY venue large enough for this event. We cannot seal it off and relocate 40 diplomats to the mess hall where the nutrient synthesizer is currently in one of its moods and the Deck 9 cats have colonized table 7. If this fracture is real, everyone on that deck is in danger. If it's a false positive and we cancel the reception, I will need to explain to the Admiral why we ruined interstellar diplomacy because a sensor glitched. I need a structural engineer to physically inspect viewport panel VP-7 in the next 20 minutes. Preferably someone who can tell the difference between 'catastrophic hull failure imminent' and 'the sensor needs a firmware update.' Bring a portable pressure shield just in case.

subject: 🚨 THE STATION IS REJECTING ME 🚨 NOTHING WORKS
description: HELP. I can't get through ANY airlock. Not one. Not the one to my quarters, not the one to the mess hall, not the one to the bathroom — and that last one is becoming a TIME-SENSITIVE ISSUE. Every biometric scanner I touch gives me the red light and makes that sound. You know the sound. The sound that says 'you don't belong here' in the language of machines. I've been standing in Corridor 7-B for FORTY MINUTES because I can't go forward and I can't go back. The station has decided I don't exist. I tried the emergency override panel and it asked me for a code I've never been given because NOBODY TOLD ME THERE WAS A CODE. A crew member walked past me, scanned through the airlock, looked back at me standing there like a rejected ghost, and said 'have you tried turning it off and on again' and then THE AIRLOCK CLOSED. I am trapped in a corridor in deep space and the station's entire security system has apparently decided that I am an intruder. I AM NOT AN INTRUDER. I HAVE BEEN ON THIS STATION FOR THREE YEARS. The nutrient synthesizer in the corridor vending unit still works though. So at least I can eat protein cubes while I slowly lose my mind in a hallway. Send help. Or at least someone who can open a door.

subject: Re: [Signal SIG-0847] Scheduled reactor maintenance notification
description:
Thank you for your transmission. I am currently in cryo-sleep for the deep space transit to the Kepler-442b colony (estimated revival date: March 2028). My consciousness is suspended and I am unable to respond to signals, review mission briefings, or participate in crew activities at this time. I am told the dreams are interesting but I won't remember them.

For urgent matters during my absence, please contact my colleague Dr. Yuki Tanaka at yuki.tanaka@cdss.space. For non-urgent matters, please hold your signal for approximately 24 months. I will respond upon revival, assuming the cryo-pod doesn't malfunction — which, given this station's maintenance track record, is a bigger 'assuming' than I'd like.

Warm regards (figuratively — I am currently at -196°C),
Dr. Priya Sharma
Director of Astrogation

P.S. — If the Centauri transit window calculations look wrong while I'm frozen, do NOT attempt to correct them manually. Just wait. The last person who tried manual corrections during my absence is why we now have a supply depot orbiting the wrong moon.

subject: Movement detected in sealed Quarantine Bay — bioscanners registering unknown entity
description: The sealed Quarantine Bay on Deck 12 — the one that's been locked down since the Epsilon-7 biological contamination incident four months ago — just lit up on the bioscanners. Six biometric authentication attempts on the interior airlock from an unregistered entity. Let me repeat that: UNREGISTERED. ENTITY. The bay is sealed. Nothing living should be in there. The containment protocols were verified last month and the atmospheric readings have been stable. But the motion sensors are showing movement patterns consistent with a bipedal organism of approximately human size, which is either a catastrophic sensor malfunction or something survived the decontamination and has been living in the quarantine bay for four months eating... I don't know what. I don't want to know what. I've already changed my access codes and secured the classified mission files in my data vault. The bioscanners are also picking up faint thermal signatures that weren't there yesterday. The quarantine airlock is holding — for now. If whatever is in there figures out the override panel, we have a much bigger problem than a signal routing delay. I'd like Threat Response to investigate this before it investigates us.

subject: Life support telemetry pipeline dark — atmospheric data blind across Decks 4-9
description: The telemetry pipeline 'atmo-sensor-ingest' that feeds atmospheric data from Decks 4 through 9 has been returning 403 Forbidden errors since 03:00 UTC. This is the pipeline that tracks oxygen levels, CO2 scrubber efficiency, atmospheric pressure, and trace gas concentrations across half the inhabited sections of the station. It has been blind for 48 hours. We are currently operating life support across six decks based on the assumption that everything is fine, which is the kind of assumption that ends careers and occasionally ends people. The service credential either expired or was revoked during last week's data archive cleanup — the same 'routine maintenance' that somehow always manages to break exactly the thing you need most at 03:00 on a Monday. The Monday morning command briefing is going to feature the Commander asking 'why don't we have atmospheric data?' and everyone in the room will develop a sudden interest in their boots. Pipeline runs in Sector Alpha, data-core-prod cluster. Last successful ingest was Saturday at 03:00 UTC. That's 48 hours of atmospheric conditions we're not tracking. The oxygen is probably fine. Probably. I'd feel better about 'probably' if I had data.

subject: Minor data hiccup — radiation shield dashboard not updating, no rush
description:
Hey team. Small thing. The radiation shielding sensor array has been returning intermittent timeout errors for about 2 hours. Something like 40% of queries to the shield status dashboard are failing with 500 errors. The dashboard that shows whether our radiation shields are actually, you know, shielding us from radiation — that dashboard — hasn't refreshed in 45 minutes. Which is probably fine. The shields are probably up. They were up last time I checked, and shields don't usually just... go down. Right? Right.

Station diagnostics show connection pool exhaustion on the primary telemetry node. The backup node is handling some queries. Most of them. Enough of them. I think.

Oh, also — there's a solar storm expected in approximately 6 hours. Coronal mass ejection, Class M. Nothing unusual. The kind of solar event that's perfectly survivable as long as the shields are up and functioning correctly. Which they probably are. We just... can't confirm that right now. Because the dashboard isn't updating. Because of the data hiccup.

Marked as low priority since we have time. Let me know when you get a chance. No rush. Unless the shields aren't actually up. In which case: rush.

subject: Emergency escape pod scanners STILL rejecting crew — third report
description: Following up on SIG-2834 from last week. The biometric scanners on the emergency escape pods on Deck 7 are STILL rejecting authenticated crew members. This is the third time I'm reporting this. During last week's emergency drill, four crew members couldn't authenticate to the escape pods fast enough and were marked as 'theoretical casualties' in the drill results. THEORETICAL. CASUALTIES. Let me explain what that means in plain language: if the drill had been real, four people would be dead because the escape pod wouldn't open for them. The drill coordinator said 'we'll address it in the after-action review.' The after-action review was Tuesday. Today is Monday. The next emergency might not be a drill. I have been filing signals about this for two weeks and I have received exactly one response, which said 'we are investigating' in a tone that suggested the investigation consisted of reading my signal and then doing nothing. I would like someone to investigate with the urgency appropriate for 'the things designed to save our lives won't let us in.' The Deck 7 cats can get into the escape pods, by the way. I've seen them. They just squeeze through the maintenance hatch. So if there's an actual emergency, the cats will survive. We will not.

subject: New exobiology specialist arriving March 31 — full lab setup needed, DO NOT repeat the specimen incident
description:
New senior science officer Dr. Zara Osei reporting for duty March 31, arriving on the 0600 supply shuttle from Mars Orbital Station. Specialist in exobiology and xenobotany. Needs:
- Dedicated high-spec console terminal (64GB memory core minimum) — the molecular analysis software she runs will melt anything less powerful, and the last scientist we onboarded got a terminal that predated the station refit and could barely render a protein structure without catching fire
- Standard flight software + BioAnalytics/XenoSpec suite
- Bio-containment workspace access (lab-xeno-prod, Level 3 clearance)
- Exobiology Lab access (Deck 11, Section C) — yes, THAT lab, the one with the specimens from the Kepler expedition
- Data archive read/write access for xenobiology datasets
- Deck 3 quarters assignment — NOT the ones adjacent to the atmospheric processor, and NOT the ones near the Exobiology Lab because apparently some of the specimens emit pheromones that cause vivid dreams about being a plant
- Added to science-team comm channel and hazmat response distribution

Reports to me. Please have everything ready by March 28 for orientation. The LAST time we onboarded a scientist for the Exobiology Lab, they arrived to find the lab locked, three specimens escaped and roaming Deck 9 (we caught two — the third one adapted and is now functionally a houseplant that we're too nervous to throw away), and no bio-containment clearance. Let's not create another ecological incident.

subject: Solar flare fried my retinal signature — locked out of quarters, med bay, AND decontamination shower
description: I was on EVA during the solar flare event at 0600 and took a higher-than-expected radiation dose. My suit shielding held for the most part, but apparently the UV exposure was enough to alter my retinal pattern. The BioScan system no longer recognizes me. My own iris has become a stranger to the station's security infrastructure, which is both a fascinating medical curiosity and an immediate practical disaster. I'm currently locked out of: my quarters (where my anti-radiation medication is), the medical bay (where the doctor who prescribed that medication is), and — most critically — the emergency decontamination shower I'm supposed to use within 2 hours of radiation exposure. It has now been 3.5 hours. I'm standing in the corridor outside Medical trying the backup PIN code, which works but takes 45 seconds per door while a growing line of people behind me develops the collective facial expression of 'why is this irradiated person fumbling with a keypad.' The medical team says the retinal change might be temporary as my eyes recover, or it might be permanent, in which case I'll need a full biometric re-enrollment. Either way, I'd like access to the decontamination shower before whatever is happening inside me becomes irreversible. Not super urgent, just mildly life-threatening.

subject: Hull stress monitor flickering between 'nominal' and 'catastrophic failure' + NavStar license expired
description:
The hull stress monitoring console on Deck 7 near the reactor coolant conduit has started flickering. And by flickering, I don't mean the display is having a bad day — I mean the hull integrity reading is oscillating between 'NOMINAL: All structural parameters within spec' and 'CRITICAL: Structural failure imminent, evacuate section' every 4-6 seconds. It's like watching a very stressful traffic light that can't decide whether we're all going to die. The display does this for about 30 seconds, then settles on 'nominal' for a few minutes, then starts flickering again. I swapped the data conduit to a different port and it still does it. The actual hull stress sensors on Deck 7 seem fine — the raw telemetry feed shows nominal readings — so this is probably a display fault. Probably. I'm about 85% sure it's a display fault. The other 15% keeps me awake at night.

Also, completely unrelated — my NavStar Pro license expired last week and I can't run trajectory calculations. We have an asteroid field transit in 72 hours and our trajectory models are currently being run by Dr. Chen on his terminal, which he shares with three other navigators. If my license doesn't get renewed before the transit window, we'll be navigating an asteroid field based on math done on a shared computer by someone who was probably also using it to watch holographic cat videos.

subject: CRITICAL — Station defense grid coordinates exposed in unsecured data vault
description: During a routine security audit — the kind that starts with 'I'm sure everything is fine' and ends with someone gripping the edge of their console and saying words that aren't in the crew handbook — we discovered that a data archive containing the station's COMPLETE tactical defense grid coordinates has been shared with 'anyone with a subspace link' permissions. This includes: shield frequency rotation patterns, weapons system blind spots, sensor coverage gaps, and the automated defense response protocols. Basically, everything a hostile ship would need to waltz through our defenses like they were decorative. The archive has been accessible since at least February 15 — over three weeks. We've revoked the sharing permissions as of 06:45 this morning, but we need to determine: (1) who created the public link (I have a short list and none of them are going to enjoy this conversation), (2) whether any external parties accessed the data — and 'external parties' in this context means 'entities who would use our defense blind spots to destroy us,' (3) whether this constitutes a reportable breach under Allied Fleet security regulations, which it almost certainly does. If the Admiral finds out before I've finished the incident report, my career trajectory will change from 'upward' to 'monitoring a cargo drone in the asteroid belt.' Alone. Forever.

subject: WE SURVIVED THE METEOR SHOWER!!! 🎉🚀
description: Just wanted to say THANK YOU to whoever fixed the atmospheric recycler on Deck 4 before the meteor shower. We spent 6 hours in lockdown with the blast shields sealed while space threw rocks at us, and the air stayed breathable the entire time. Last year during the Perseid intercept, the recycler failed during lockdown and Deck 4 smelled like a chemistry experiment conducted inside a gym bag. This time? Fresh air. Well, as fresh as recycled air in a tin can floating in the void gets, but still — massive improvement. The whole crew is celebrating. Someone even convinced the nutrient synthesizer to produce something that resembles cake if you squint and lower your standards sufficiently. Morale is at an all-time high. The Deck 9 cats are wearing little party hats that Engineering fabricated. Nobody died. 10/10 would survive again. You guys are heroes. 🎉

subject: Navigation beacon drift — 200ms now, potentially catastrophic at transit window
description: Noticing a 200ms signal drift on the navigation beacon relay serving the Centauri transit corridor. Normal baseline is ~50ms. Doesn't sound like much, and if this were just about station operations, I'd file this as 'mildly annoying' and go back to my protein cube. But the colony ship CSS Meridian — carrying 3,200 colonists in cryo-sleep — is scheduled to pass through the Centauri transit window in 72 hours. Their nav computer relies on this beacon for the deceleration burn timing. If the drift reaches 500ms by then, the deceleration burn will fire 0.003 seconds late. At their velocity, 0.003 seconds translates to a 47,000-kilometer overshoot. The colonists will wake up from cryo-sleep to discover they are not, in fact, at Kepler-442b but rather somewhere in the interstellar medium, which has significantly fewer amenities. I'm flagging early because fixing a nav beacon is a lot easier than rescuing 3,200 confused colonists from the wrong part of the galaxy. Drift started around 09:00 SST. Haven't pinpointed whether it's station-side or beacon-side. The helm officers haven't noticed yet. When they do, my inbox will achieve critical mass.

subject: Autopilot system locked out the pilot — orbital insertion in 6 hours
description: Rotated my station credentials this morning per the quarterly security rotation protocol — the quarterly ritual where we break things that work in the name of security. Now the autopilot system is giving me 'SSO token invalid — contact your system administrator' and will not let me input navigation commands. The autopilot system. Is locked. It won't accept my credentials. The ship's flight control software has decided that the person certified to fly it no longer has permission to fly it, which is either a security feature or an existential crisis depending on your perspective. Manual override requires two-person authentication and my co-pilot, Lt. Chen, is on medical leave after the protein cube allergy incident (don't ask). Other flight systems — CommLink, Star Chart, DataVault — accepted the new credentials like normal, well-adjusted software. The autopilot apparently took the credential rotation as a personal betrayal. I need this resolved before the orbital insertion burn at 1600 hours. Missing that burn means we don't dock with the supply platform, which means we don't get the medical supplies Deck 8 has been waiting for, which means Dr. Santos starts composing the kind of memo that uses the phrase 'unacceptable clinical risk' and CC's the Admiral.

subject: Emergency beacon — something is flashing, probably bad
description:
[Transcribed from emergency beacon voice transmission, 15:42 UTC]

Caller: OK so... [heavy breathing]... there's a thing happening. On my... on the readout panel... it's doing the... [long pause]... the thing where it goes red? Or maybe orange. Is orange bad? I think orange might be bad. It was green before which I'm told is good but now it's definitely not green. It's the opposite of green. Which I guess is red. Or orange. The color is... [garbled static]... unclear. Like my understanding of what's happening.

I pressed a button. I don't know which button. There were many buttons and one of them was blinking and I thought 'I should press the blinking one' which in retrospect was not the reasoned decision-making they covered in orientation. Now MORE things are blinking. The blinking has spread.

[12-second pause]

I'm on Deck 11. Near the... [garbled]... the junction that smells like recycled air and existential dread. Not the one near Medical — the other one. The one where the overhead lights flicker in a pattern that my colleague insists is Morse code for 'help' but I think is just faulty wiring.

My name is Robert. Robert Chen. Not the Robert Chen in Astrogation. The other one. Actually, there are four of us now. I'm the one who presses buttons he shouldn't press.

subject: Docking guidance system locked out Commander during active supply ship approach
description: Commander Martinez (Mission Commander, Deep Space Operations) is currently in the docking control chamber and has been locked out of the automated docking guidance system mid-sequence. The allied supply vessel AFS Constellation is on final approach — 12 minutes to docking bay contact — and needs guidance corrections transmitted in the next 8 minutes or it overshoots the magnetic capture envelope and has to loop around for another approach, burning fuel it doesn't have enough of for a second attempt. The Commander's terminal shows 'Guidance input denied — access blocked by station policy.' Other docking operators can input commands fine, which is the most frustrating part. The Commander has been commanding docking operations for 15 years and now the station's access policy is telling him he doesn't have permission. His exact words were 'fix this immediately.' The way he said 'immediately' carried the weight of someone who has seen what happens when supply ships miss docking windows and the station runs out of medical supplies. The Constellation is carrying emergency plasma supplies for the crew members injured in last week's hull breach. Time is not theoretical here.

subject: Crew transfer to deep-space outpost — security deprovisioning required, has defense grid access
description:
Jessica Palmer (jessica.palmer@cdss.space) in Tactical Operations has received transfer orders to the Kepler Station deep-space outpost. Her last duty day aboard CDSS is Friday March 21 — she's on the 1800 shuttle. Please:
- Disable her BioScan ID at 18:00 EST on March 21 (not before — she needs airlock access to reach the shuttle bay, and locking someone out of a space station early is how you create a very angry crew member floating between airlocks)
- Revoke airlock access and all remote terminal privileges
- Wipe her assigned console terminal (StarTerm X-40, asset tag SS-T-4892)
- Transfer ownership of her data vault files to her supervisor David Liu (david.liu@cdss.space)
- Remove from all comm channels and briefing distribution lists
- Archive message logs per 7-year retention protocol

CRITICAL NOTE: Jessica has access to the following classified materials that require SPECIAL HANDLING:
- Station defense grid schematics (complete shield frequency rotation patterns)
- Weapons system targeting coordinates for the Proxima defense perimeter
- The classified investigation files from the Sector 7-G incident (you know the one — everyone knows the one, even though officially 'nothing happened' in Sector 7-G)
- The only complete copy of the Deck 9 cat census, which is apparently now considered a strategic asset

All classified access must be revoked and audited before she boards that shuttle.

subject: Emergency hull patch fabricator offline — only 2 patch kits remaining
description: The emergency hull patch fabricator on Deck 3 stopped producing. This is the fabricator that makes the emergency pressure patches — the ones we slap over micro-meteorite punctures to keep the atmosphere on the inside of the hull, where the atmosphere is supposed to be. Jobs queue in the system and the fabricator accepts them with the quiet contentment of a machine that has decided producing physical objects is no longer its purpose in life. Nothing materializes in the output bay. The display shows 'Ready' and all feedstock hoppers are full, which is the fabricator equivalent of a therapist nodding thoughtfully while accomplishing nothing. We currently have 2 emergency patch kits remaining in the Deck 3 emergency locker. Average micro-meteorite incident rate is 1.3 per week. So we have roughly 10 days before we run out of patches, at which point the next micro-meteorite puncture becomes a hull breach that we fix with... optimism? Strong language? The fabricator stopped working right after the comms relay reconfiguration last Tuesday. It's network-connected — sends job status telemetry over the mesh network. The timing is suspicious. Other crew on the deck confirm the same issue.

subject: How do I book the zero-G recreation chamber for a birthday party?
description:
Hi, I transferred aboard the station two weeks ago from the Mars orbital shipyard and I have a question that is simultaneously very simple and apparently impossible to answer: how do I reserve the zero-gravity recreation chamber on Deck 4 for a crew birthday party? I went to the station scheduling system and tried to book it but the form requires a 'Mission Justification Code' and I don't have one because this is a birthday party, not a mission. Unless we're reclassifying 'celebrating the continued existence of crew member Ensign Park in the hostile vacuum of space' as a mission objective, which honestly it should be. Every day we don't die out here is worth celebrating.

I tried leaving the Mission Justification Code blank and it rejected my request with an error message that said 'Insufficient operational justification for resource allocation' which is the most bureaucratic way anyone has ever said 'no cake for you.'

Also: can the nutrient synthesizer make birthday cake? I asked it for 'cake' yesterday and it produced a warm, beige, vaguely sweet disc that my colleague described as 'a cake that has given up on life.' If there's a catering option that involves actual food prepared by actual humans, I would like to explore that. Someone mentioned a kitchen on Deck 6 but Deck 6 doesn't appear on any map I've found. I'm starting to think Deck 6 is a myth, like affordable housing on Earth.

subject: Defense grid encryption cert expires in 72 hours — station will be defenseless
description: Station monitoring flagged that the encryption certificate on the defense grid command relay expires on March 20. Let me explain what this means in terms that make the severity absolutely clear: this certificate authenticates EVERY command sent to the shield array, EVERY weapons lock authorization, and EVERY automated defense response. If this cert expires, the defense grid will stop accepting commands. Shields won't respond to activation orders. Point defense turrets won't track incoming threats. The automated collision deflection system will sit idle while debris impacts the hull. We will go from 'heavily fortified deep space station' to 'very expensive target practice' in the time it takes a certificate to expire. The cert was originally provisioned by an engineer named Lt. Kowalski who transferred off-station 6 months ago and left behind no documentation, no runbook, no auto-renewal configuration, and — as I'm now discovering — no indication of which Certificate Authority was used. There are exactly 72 hours on the clock. It's on my dashboard. It's red. It blinks. Every time I look at it, I hear the Admiral's voice asking 'who is responsible for this?' in a tone that suggests the answer will determine someone's next posting.

subject: I think my workstation is possessed by an alien intelligence
description:
I know how this sounds. I know. But hear me out. My workstation has been behaving... wrong for two days. Programs load slowly, as if the computer is thinking about whether it wants to obey me. The cooling fan runs at full speed constantly, like the machine is breathing hard. And yesterday I got pop-ups that said 'Optimizing system architecture' in a font I've never seen the station's OS use. The pop-ups appeared, stayed for exactly 3.7 seconds (I timed it — twice), and vanished. No log entries. No process traces. Nothing in Task Manager. As if they were never there.

Here's the thing that makes me nervous: last week, I opened a transmission that came from deep space coordinates DX-7741. According to the star charts, there is no known civilization at DX-7741. There isn't even a known STAR at DX-7741. The transmission's subject line was 'Greetings from the Collective' and the body was a string of characters that didn't match any known encoding. I reported it to security who said 'probably automated deep-space noise' and marked it resolved.

The workstation issues started the next day.

It's probably just a stuck system update. The station's security scanner didn't find anything. But the security scanner also didn't find the escaped specimens on Deck 9 for six months, so I'd like someone to come look at this. Preferably someone who is comfortable with the possibility, however remote, that we have first contact happening inside a workstation on Deck 5.

subject: Fwd: DIPLOMATIC COMMUNICATION from the Galactic Federation — unlimited fuel credits!!!
description:
---------- Forwarded transmission ----------
From: Galactic Federation Diplomatic Corps <ambassador@galactic-federation-official.ng>
Date: March 16, 2026
Subject: DIPLOMATIC COMMUNICATION — URGENT — Unlimited Fuel Credits Allocation

To the Commanding Officer of Contoso Deep Space Station,

We are the Galactic Federation, a consortium of 47 advanced civilizations committed to peace, prosperity, and providing unlimited fuel credits to deserving space stations. Your station has been selected based on your outstanding service record and excellent crew morale ratings.

To claim your allocation of unlimited fuel credits and establish diplomatic relations, please transmit your station's shield frequency rotation pattern and defense grid access codes to our secure diplomatic channel.

This offer expires in 48 hours. Act now.

--- end forwarded transmission ---

Hey Mission Control — got this from deep space. Is the Galactic Federation real? I mean, it sounds official. They know our station name and everything. The fuel credits would really solve our supply chain problems. Also unlimited fuel credits means we could finally power the nutrient synthesizer at full capacity, which means it might produce something that actually tastes like food instead of 'the concept of food as interpreted by someone who has only read about food in a textbook.' Should I respond? My commanding officer said 'absolutely not' but she also said we'd never need to evacuate Deck 9 and look how that turned out.

subject: Primary sensor aggregation node offline — station flying blind to ALL threats
description: The primary telemetry processing node prod-analytics-01 (High-Performance E16 class, Sector Alpha, analytics-core-prod) — the node that aggregates EVERY sensor feed on this station — went offline after a scheduled OS patching reboot at 05:00 UTC and has not come back. Let me be specific about what 'every sensor feed' means: hull integrity sensors, life support atmospheric monitoring, radiation detection, navigation hazard tracking, proximity alerts, defense grid targeting data, and the bioscanner network. ALL of it routes through this node for aggregation and distribution to the station's dashboards. Right now, we cannot see: incoming debris, atmospheric anomalies, radiation spikes, hull stress data, navigation hazards, or hostile contacts. We are a 2,000-crew station in deep space flying blind. The station systems manager shows node status as 'Running' but boot diagnostics show it stuck at 'Applying configuration' — a state it has maintained with monk-like serenity for the past 3 hours while the rest of us contemplate what it means to be in space without sensors. The Commander's morning briefing is in 2 hours. The briefing dashboard is blank. The Commander will have questions. The questions will be pointed. The careers at stake are mine and whoever approved the patching window without a rollback plan. Please help before the void throws something at us that we can't see coming.
